turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: In case number 16-980, Husted v. A. Philip Randolph Institute, Justice Alito has the opinion of the court.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Under the Constitution, the States have the primary responsibility for keeping the list of registered voters up to date. This includes maintaining current addresses for all registered voters in the State and that is not an easy task. More than 10% of all Americans move every year and it has been estimated that some 24 million voter registrations about one in eight are either invalid or significantly inaccurate. Before us today is an Ohio program that aims to keep the state's voting list up to date by removing the names of those who have moved out of the district where they are registered to vote. Ohio uses the failure to engage in any voting activity for two years as a rough way of identifying voters who may have moved. It then sends these individuals a pre-addressed postage prepaid card asking them to verify that they still reside at the same address. Voters who return the card are kept on rolls. Voters who do not return the card are not automatically removed from the rolls if they vote at any time in any election for the next four years that their registration is maintained; however, if they do not vote for an additional four-year period, their names are removed from the rolls and they will have to reregister if they wish to vote. We are asked to decide whether this Ohio program complies with Federal Law and we hold that it does. The two federal laws at issue, the National Voter Registration Act and the Help America Vote Act prescribe requirements for State removal programs like Ohio's. Most important here, these laws tell the states that they may not remove anyone from the rolls on change of residence grounds unless the states send registrants a return card notifying them how to set stay on the rolls. States may then remove these registrants if they fail to mail back the card and fail to vote for an additional four years. This is exactly what Ohio does. Because Ohio follows these requirements to the letter, its program is permitted by federal law. Respondents argue and the Sixth Circuit held that even if Ohio's process complies with these statutory requirements it nevertheless violates a different part of federal law known as the Failure to Vote Clause. This clause prohibits states from removing someone from the rolls "by reason of the person's failure to vote". Respondents point out that Ohio's procedure uses a person's failure to vote twice; once as a trigger for sending out the return cards and again as one of the two requirements for removal. They therefore argue that Ohio's use of nonvoting is illegal. We disagree, read in context the Failure to Vote Clause simply forbids the use of nonvoting as the sole criterion for removing a registrant from the rolls. Another provision of Federal Law in HAVA America Vote Act puts it exactly that way, uses the term solely, and Ohio does not use nonvoting as the sole reason for removing a registrant from the list. Instead as expressly permitted by federal law Ohio removes registrants only if they fail to vote and have failed to respond to a notice. Therefore, Ohio has not removed anybody solely for failing to vote and it has thus not violated this clause. Respondents have several policy disagreements with Ohio and with Congress, but this case presents a question of statutory interpretation not a question of policy. We do not have the authority to second-guess the removal methods that Congress approved or to decide whether Ohio's process is the ideal method for keeping its voting rolls up to date. The only question before us and the only question we decide is whether Ohio violates federal law. For the reasons explained and the others set out in our opinion, it does not. We therefore reverse the contrary judgment of the Sixth Circuit. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Breyer has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justices Ginsburg, Sotomayor and Kagan joined. Justice Sotomayor has also filed a dissenting opinion.
